---
title: "ECNS 560 Project Report"
author: "Cooper Craig and Nicholas Starring"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: lumen
---
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,      # Hide code
  error = FALSE,     # Hide error messages
  warning = FALSE,   # Hide warnings (like "NAs introduced")
  message = FALSE,   # Hide package loading messages
  fig.align = "center" # Centers all your plots automatically
)
```

# Project Introduction, Topic, and Motivation

### Topic and Motivation

For this project, we aim to better understand recessions and develop a potential indicator. A recession is usually defined as two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth. More generally, recessions (or similar events) may be thought of as when the economy goes beyond "slowing down" and begins shrinking in some aspect. This is, or should be, of interest to almost every economic agent, from policymakers and business leaders to households and individuals. A recession can have many impacts, but may generally be classified into two categories: either a "financial crisis" or a "labor recession." It is clear that a financial crisis is of interest to everyone with assets—if the value of assets is decreasing, then it is reasonable to expect that there will be massive income effects as these agents "feel poorer" and respond rationally. If there is a labor recession, households are likely to struggle to provide enough labor to satisfy their needs, and policymakers would like to mitigate this.

Existing predictors of recessions are commonly flawed and overfit—there is a saying that "economists have predicted 11 of the last 5 recessions." This stems from identifying some condition which has occurred prior to most or all of the recessions observed in the United States based on a single (or a relatively small number) indicator variable. Perhaps the most famous of these is the inversion of the yield curve on US treasury notes. While this may occur prior to most recessions, it is a sentiment-based indicator rather than a structural indicator. Further, it is imprecise, commonly referenced as indicating a recession sometime in the next 6–12 months. This presents obvious issues, as regular false positives and an uncertain timeline prevent preparatory or preventative actions from being reasonably taken.

The metric we propose reduces this dependence on so little information. It is not particularly obvious how a good predictor might be constructed; one of the most significant challenges is the lack of data, as recessions occur somewhat infrequently. This makes any attempt at prediction subject to overfitting, though we aim to construct robustness via out-of-sample testing—a necessary characteristic of the machine learning methods we use.

### Principal Methodology: PH and Wasserstein Distance

We attempt to understand whether a recession can be signaled by a change in the "velocity" of the economy, and we define this velocity as the Wasserstein distance between two consecutive states. To achieve this, we use two techniques not yet assimilated into mainstream empirical economics: Persistent Homology (PH) and the Wasserstein distance.

#### Persistent Homology (PH)
PH is a tool which models the "shape" of data—how discrete data points are embedded in a high-dimensional manifold in space—and allows for unsupervised clustering and structuring. In detail, PH studies emergent structures in the data and at what observational scale these structures exist. It does this by setting a ball of radius $r$ around each point in space, then varying the size of $r$. When these balls overlap, an edge is added between them. This forms a graph, where the data points are the vertices and the edges are a function of the size of the radius. If PH is taken further, there are additional degrees where it may be extended: if three points are fully connected, then they enclose a triangular "face", and if four points are fully connected, then they enclose a tetrahedronal volume. This space-filling volume may be taken to whatever extent desired, which is the study of simplicial complexes. For the purpose of this study, we stop at the two-dimensional faces, so consider only the edges and faces.

#### Wasserstein Distance
The Wasserstein distance is a measure of optimal transport between two structures. It is commonly known as the "earth-mover's distance," as it measures the distance between two probability distributions via minimal shifting of this probability—hence the comparison to moving earth, where this distance is the amount of dirt which must be moved to make pile number (1) equivalent in shape to pile number (2). In a physics sense, this is a measure of work, and in a more purely mathematical sense, it functions as a topological derivative—the rate of change between topological states.

We create a time series of this distance as we move a window of length $k$ along the data, creating PH structures for windows containing time steps 1:k, 2:(k+1), ..., n:(n+k-1). The overlap in these windows means that measuring the distance between consecutive structures is a measure of the difference in influence of the oldest (exiting) point and the newest (entering) point. If these points are identical, the PH structures are identical and the distance is zero. If they are strongly influential in different aspects, this distance is very large. It is our hypothesis that this distance will act as an edge detector between states, and in particular, the entry or exit from a recessionary economic state.

### Evaluation Metrics: ROC and AUC

Given the above methodology, we use Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis and Area Under the Curve (AUC) as evaluation metrics.

ROC compares the false positives and true positives of a binary classifier. For this metric, some threshold $\tau$ is chosen for the Wasserstein metric; if the metric is greater than $\tau$, then a recession is signaled. This charts the sensitivity (true positive rate) on the Y-axis and the complement of the specificity (false positive rate) on the X-axis. This curve will necessarily form an arc between (0,0), where the model detects no recessions (due to a threshold set too high), and (1,1), where the model signals everything as a recession (due to a threshold set too low). Assuming a symmetric cost of misclassification, the optimal threshold value $\tau^*$ is chosen as the $\tau$ corresponding to the point on this curve closest to (0,1), for perfect detection of true positives without any false indications.

The AUC is the area under the ROC. It measures the overall quality of the signal from this model on a scale from 0 to 1; we can interpret this as the probability that this model will assign a higher Wasserstein distance to a recessionary window than to a non-recessionary window. This should be within the interval (0.5, 1), because if it is worse than random, we would simply invert the signal. This metric is less sensitive to the potential to "get lucky" with careful selection of the threshold $\tau$ than many alternatives, because it considers all thresholds and the power of the model over all variations of this parameter. Further, as a metric derived from ROC, the AUC is invariant on the partition of the data between classes, and so will not be artificially inflated by an imbalance between them in the training data.

# Data and Pre-Processing

Our data is retrieved from Yahoo Finance (via the "tidyquant" R package) and from FRED (also via the "tidyquant" R package). This data includes time series for the daily close of major stock indices (S&P 500, DOW, NASDAQ, Russel 2000), common hedges for uncertainty (gold, silver, Case-Schiller Index for real estate), and monthly data on macroeconomic data (U3 and U6 unemployment, GDP, CPI, Federal Funds Rate, Housing Starts, ...) (A full data appendix is found in the [*clean_data.R*]{.underline} script). Additionally, API sourcing is used to collect two measures of consumer sentiment around recessions; google search trend data for "recession" and wikipedia page visits for "recession", to better obtain a sort of "panic" measure that may be useful in predicting recessions. Collectively, these present a multi-faceted image of the economy at a point in time -- in particular, they represent both classical macroeconomic indicators and aggregating signals for the "shadow economy" of asset prices and volatility hedges. Further, the vast majority of our data begins well before 2000, with some stretching back to the beginning of the 20th century, a visualization attached below will demonstrate that. Recession data is manually collected into a dataframe for easy joining with the larger dataset, the data is from the NBER. Lagged indicators for if a recession starts in the next 6, 12 and 18 months are created from this data and will serve as our target predicted variable in terms of forecasting recessions.

The vast majority of our data is observed monthly, therefore, we collapse our more granular data (like asset prices) to a monthly level, creating a monthly mean close price and an interday volatility measure from the variance of these daily closing prices within the month. This collapsed data is combined with the other data, merging on the **primary key of year-month**, at which point **a single primary key** for our cleaned dataset is created.

Additionally, linear imputation was performed on the consumer sentiment variable, which was observed quarterly until the late 1970's, following this though, it too is observed on a monthly basis.

It is also worth noting again that there is a "data appendix" in the "clean_data.R" script containing much more detailed descriptions of each variable and its source.

From this, we do some further processing, to create a relative-scaled first-difference dataset. Importantly, this relative-scaled first-difference is computed as a direct first-difference for those values which are already in percentage terms (interest rates or unemployment), and similarly, we also use this direct first-differencing for mean-reverting series, to avoid seeing explosive growth around zero or negligible percent changes for large level-valued changes as it reaches peaks or troughs. Both the transformed and the untransformed data can be useful, but the first difference and relative scaling normalizes and whitens the data, reducing issues of autocorrelation and scale differences between variables. These transformations are also then formally normalized, as that is what persistent homology requires to work properly.

Finally, we append some functions of our variables onto that dataset to allow for a more robust approach when using persistent homology. In particular, we use log-returns for our asset data and other compounding indices, which tend to exhibit exponential growth and would thus distort the spacial "shape" of the data we intend to exploit in such a manner as to dominate any further processes, despite its failure as a dominant indicator of recessions.

In terms of missing data, as is addressed in the visualizations section of this report, the only missingness of concern is the lack of duration that some features have data for. Aside from that, missing values are of little concern at this stage, keeping in mind our temporal restrictions.

To ensure the robustness and validity of the data, an analysis of outliers is also carried out on the data, using the *dlookr* package in R. Using this tool, we discovered that while there are extreme values, they are naturally occurring, as most can be explained by looking at the historical period in which they lay. This analysis also shed light to the variability of some of our variables, with certain ones being composed of \~35% outlier observations. These outliers form the basis for the detection of recessions, with the idea that recessions will generally be associated with economic extremum.

These steps were carried out in the direction of the "**data cleaning checklist**", which is applied completely in the "clean_data.R" script, though due to the formalized and clean nature of the data we bring in, the data is quite "clean" already.

## Analysis of Macroeconomic Data

Prior to processing our data into topological structures, to predict recessions it is important to know if we have ground to stand on in the first place. In that, we need to determine if these variables, and their relationships meaningfully differ when the economy is in a recession, or near one.

We do so by choosing two representative variables, that are often named as some of the most important macroeconomic indicators; the federal funds rate and the u3 unemployment rate. We restrict our exploratory data analysis to just these variables since doing every subset of the variables in our dataset would be impractical, and it is not unrealistic to think that the lessons learned from just these variables can be applied to many of the others in our data set owing to the theoretical interconnecitvity of all of our variables.

With each of these two variables, conditional distributions are created, where we look to see if the distribution of either variable is notably different depending on if the month of observation was recessionary or not. For both variables, whether looking at levels or measures of monthly change, the distributions are different in respect to their month's recessionary status, for example,

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="70%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="All of the plots are included in the MacroVars_EDA.R script"}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/fedfunds_level_by_regime.png")
```

These findings give us foundational reasoning to suspect that there are different, detectable regimes with these variables depending on if the economy is in a recession or not, giving credence to the idea that recessionary periods have different topological structures than stable periods do. This implyies that our Wasserstein metric prediction method may have some weight behind it. But again, this is very cursory evidence, and shouldn't be seen as definitive in any sort.

To gain more momentum behind our topological approach, it is also of use to see if the relationships between these two variables changes dependent on recessionary status, as this is ultimately what our topological approach hinges upon. We plot the two variables against eachother, both in levels and rates, to see if their relationship changes dependent on economic stability, and find visual evidence across both levels and rates that there is some change in the relationship, particularly when looking at if the period prior to a recession (as that is ultimately what we want to predict-if we're in a month with a recession upcoming). If there were no regime-effect, we'd see the red dots evenly distrbuted among the grey dots.

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="70%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="All of the plots are included in the MacroVars_EDA.R script"}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/scatter_fedfunds_u3_levels_lag12.png")
```

We also examine how the two variables vary together through time by looking at a joint time series between the two against shaded recession periods. These plots show a slight relationship, and but certainly nothing that is visibly noteworthy. These plots are interactive and are found in the MacroVars_EDA.R script. They are not included here to reduce cluttering, keeping in mind the rubric.

We also conduct an analysis of extreme values for these two variables and find notable outliers for both, but since both are true, factual data, which can be explained by events at the time, they are retained. Their retention is also necessary when considering our topological approach, as the Wasserstein distances will be driven by large shocks that an outlier causes. This extreme value analysis was then expanded to every variable, and as touched on in the first section of this report, no outliers will be excluded from analysis, as they are all "real" datum, and drive our Wasserstein TDA approach.

## Visualizations

This plot illustrates what periods we do have data for, and where it is missing. Most of the data is present and once the data series starts, it is almost perfectly complete, with some sparse missing values around 2025 due to the government shutdown.

### Data Coverage /Missing Values

```{r data-coverage, fig.cap="Data Coverage and Missingness Map"}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/data_coverage_timeline.png")
```

This graph illustrates the general completeness of our data, and once the time series starts, it is generally complete -- barring the month or two of missing data at the end of 2025, due to the aforementioned government shutdown.

### 3-D Tracking of points through space

These plots show the path through time the US economy has followed, where color illustrates the time variable, on several three-axis subsets of our data. In particular, we can see that there are some patterns and regions that this path seems to end up in -- our goal is to identify some repeating area where recessions occur, and use that pattern or spacial region to predict future recessions.

```{r}

```

### Time series for most variables

Here, we can see how several variable change through time, as well as visualizing when the series starts. This plot allows us to get a better idea of the trends of some of our variables of interest, in order to better inform our understanding of the topological objects created through persistent homology.

```         
```

### Distributions for Variables of Interest

These plots show the distribution of several variables.

```         
```
# Results

## Analysis of Topological Data

As is outlined in the introduction, our analysis examines the distance between topological structures from one time window to the next using the Wasserstein metric (which are calculated for each dimension of topological structure in a given window). These distances will be the variables that will be used to predict if a month is near an upcoming recession or not. A more thorough description of our process is found in the methodology section.

With that said, due to unequal data coverage, computational limitations, and lessons from other similar empirical approaches, we do not and cannot use our full data in our TDA approach (Gidea & Katz, 2018; Ismail et al. 2022). Instead, we select multiple groupings of variables that combine both a good amount of data availability (the data availability for a group will only be as good as its "most missing" member variable), and theoretical relevance to recessions. These included groupings of "monetary movers" (yields on 1 year and 10 year treasuries and the federal funds rate), "pre-depression specification" (those variables for which the )
